--- SOURCE 1: Brain-Computer Interface - an overview | ScienceDirect Topics ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/neuroscience/brain-computer-interface

SUMMARY:
The brain–computer interface (BCI) is a system that detects brain neural signals and analyzes and converts them to commands that are transmitted to output



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Brain–computer interface - Wikipedia ---
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface

SUMMARY:
A **brain–computer interface** (**BCI**), sometimes called a **brain–machine interface** (**BMI**), is a direct communication link between the brain's electrical activity and an external device, most commonly a computer or robotic limb. After initial studies in rats during the 1990s, Nicolelis and colleagues developed BCIs that decoded brain activity in owl monkeys and used the devices to reproduce monkey movements in robotic arms.

FULL CONTENT:

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

For direct brain control of prosthetic devices, see [Neuroprosthetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroprosthetics "Neuroprosthetics").

Direct communication pathway between an enhanced or wired brain and an external device

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/BrainGate.jpg/250px-BrainGate.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:BrainGate.jpg)

Dummy unit illustrating the design of a [BrainGate](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/BrainGate "BrainGate") interface

A **brain–computer interface** (**BCI**), sometimes called a **brain–machine interface** (**BMI**), is a direct communication link between the [brain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain "Brain")'s electrical activity and an external device, most commonly a computer or robotic limb. BCIs are often directed at researching, [mapping](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain_mapping "Brain mapping"), assisting, [augmenting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Augmented_cognition "Augmented cognition"), or repairing human [cognitive](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cognitive_skill "Cognitive skill") or [sensory-motor functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sensory-motor_coupling "Sensory-motor coupling").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-Krucoff_584-1) They are often conceptualized as a [human–machine interface](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Human%E2%80%93machine_interface "Human–machine interface") that skips the intermediary of moving body parts (e.g. hands or feet). BCI implementations range from non-invasive ([EEG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/EEG "EEG"), [MEG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetoencephalography "Magnetoencephalography"), [MRI](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/MRI "MRI")) and partially invasive ([ECoG](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ECoG "ECoG") and endovascular) to invasive ([microelectrode array](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microelectrode_array "Microelectrode array")), based on how physically close electrodes are to brain tissue.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-:7-2)

Research on BCIs began in the 1970s by Jacques Vidal at the [University of California, Los Angeles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_California,_Los_Angeles "University of California, Los Angeles") (UCLA) under a grant from the [National Science Foundation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), followed by a contract from the [Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DARPA "DARPA") (DARPA).[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-Vidal1-3)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-Vidal2-4) Vidal's 1973 paper introduced the expression _brain–computer interface_ into scientific literature.

Due to the [cortical plasticity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cortical_plasticity "Cortical plasticity") of the brain, signals from implanted [prostheses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prostheses "Prostheses") can, after adaptation, be handled by the brain like natural sensor or effector channels.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-5) Following years of animal experimentation, the first [neuroprosthetic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroprosthetic "Neuroprosthetic") devices were implanted in humans in the mid-1990s.

History
-------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The history of brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) starts with [Hans Berger](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hans_Berger "Hans Berger")'s discovery of the brain's electrical activity and the development of [electroencephalography](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electroencephalography "Electroencephalography") (EEG). In 1924 Berger was the first to record human brain activity utilizing EEG. Berger was able to identify [oscillatory activity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neural_oscillation "Neural oscillation"), such as the [alpha wave](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha_wave "Alpha wave") (8–13 Hz), by analyzing EEG traces.

Berger's first recording device was rudimentary. He inserted [silver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Silver "Silver") wires under the scalps of his patients. These were later replaced by silver foils attached to the patient's head by rubber bandages. Berger connected these sensors to a [Lippmann capillary electrometer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lippmann_electrometer "Lippmann electrometer"), with disappointing results. However, more sophisticated measuring devices, such as the [Siemens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Siemens "Siemens") double-coil recording [galvanometer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Galvanometer "Galvanometer"), which displayed [voltages](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voltages "Voltages") as small as 10−4 volt, led to success.

Berger analyzed the interrelation of alternations in his EEG wave diagrams with [brain diseases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain_diseases "Brain diseases"). EEGs permitted completely new possibilities for brain research.

Although the term had not yet been coined, one of the earliest examples of a working brain-machine interface was the piece _Music for Solo Performer_ (1965) by American composer [Alvin Lucier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alvin_Lucier "Alvin Lucier"). The piece makes use of EEG and [analog signal processing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Analog_signal_processing "Analog signal processing") hardware (filters, amplifiers, and a mixing board) to stimulate acoustic percussion instruments. Performing the piece requires producing [alpha waves](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alpha_waves "Alpha waves") and thereby "playing" the various instruments via loudspeakers that are placed near or directly on the instruments.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-6)

[Jacques Vidal](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Jacques_Vidal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jacques Vidal (page does not exist)") coined the term "BCI" and produced the first peer-reviewed publications on this topic.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-Vidal1-3)[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-Vidal2-4) He is widely recognized as the inventor of BCIs.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-Wolpaw,_J.R_2012-7)[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-8)[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-9) A review pointed out that Vidal's 1973 paper stated the "BCI challenge"[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-Bozinovski1-10) of controlling external objects using EEG signals, and especially use of [Contingent Negative Variation (CNV)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Contingent_negative_variation "Contingent negative variation") potential as a challenge for BCI control. Vidal's 1977 experiment was the first application of BCI after his 1973 BCI challenge. It was a noninvasive EEG (actually [Visual Evoked Potentials](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evoked_potential "Evoked potential") (VEP)) control of a cursor-like graphical object on a computer screen. The demonstration was movement in a maze.[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-11)

1988 was the first demonstration of noninvasive EEG control of a physical object, a robot. The experiment demonstrated EEG control of multiple start-stop-restart cycles of movement, along an arbitrary trajectory defined by a line drawn on a floor. The line-following behavior was the default robot behavior, utilizing autonomous intelligence and an autonomous energy source.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-12)[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-13)[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-14)[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-15)

In 1990, a report was given on a closed loop, bidirectional, adaptive BCI controlling a computer buzzer by an anticipatory brain potential, the Contingent Negative Variation (CNV) potential.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-16)[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-17) The experiment described how an expectation state of the brain, manifested by CNV, used a feedback loop to control the S2 buzzer in the S1-S2-CNV paradigm. The resulting cognitive wave representing the expectation learning in the brain was termed Electroexpectogram (EXG). The CNV brain potential was part of Vidal's 1973 challenge.

Studies in the 2010s suggested neural stimulation's potential to restore functional connectivity and associated behaviors through modulation of molecular mechanisms.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-18)[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-19) This opened the door for the concept that BCI technologies may be able to restore function.

Beginning in 2013, [DARPA](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DARPA "DARPA") funded BCI technology through the BRAIN initiative, which supported work out of teams including [University of Pittsburgh Medical Center](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/University_of_Pittsburgh_Medical_Center "University of Pittsburgh Medical Center"),[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-20) Paradromics,[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-21) Brown,[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-22) and Synchron.[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-23)

Neuroprosthetics
----------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Neuroprosthetics")]

Main article: [Neuroprosthetics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroprosthetics "Neuroprosthetics")

Neuroprosthetics is an area of [neuroscience](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroscience "Neuroscience") concerned with neural prostheses, that is, using artificial devices to replace the function of impaired nervous systems and brain-related problems, or of sensory or other organs (bladder, diaphragm, etc.). As of December 2010, [cochlear implants](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cochlear_implants "Cochlear implants") had been implanted as neuroprosthetic devices in some 736,900 people worldwide.[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-24) Other neuroprosthetic devices aim to restore vision, including [retinal implants](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Retinal_implant "Retinal implant"). The first neuroprosthetic device, however, was the [pacemaker](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pacemaker "Pacemaker").

The terms are sometimes used interchangeably. Neuroprosthetics and BCIs seek to achieve the same aims, such as restoring sight, hearing, movement, ability to communicate, and even [cognitive function](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cognitive_function "Cognitive function").[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Brain%E2%80%93computer_interface#cite_note-Krucoff_584-1) Both use similar experimental methods and surgical techniques.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Investigate the various BCI systems that detect and interpret brain signals, such as EEG, ECoG, and invasive microelectrode arrays, facilitating direct communication with devices.
Examine BCI applications in neuroprosthetics, communication aids for disabled individuals, gaming, and potential uses in cognitive enhancement and mental health treatments.
Address current challenges in BCI research, including signal noise, ethical concerns, and the need for improved technology, while outlining promising future advancements and applications.
